els.name: Title and Territorial Extent
els.desc: S.1 gives the Code its short title — the Pakistan Penal Code — and declares it shall take effect throughout Pakistan. This came into force on 6th October 1860. It establishes the Code as the primary substantive criminal law applicable across all of Pakistan.
els.legal: S.1 is the foundation of all jurisdiction. The phrase 'throughout Pakistan' means the Code applies uniformly in all provinces and territories. There is no geographic carve-out within Pakistan. Any offence committed anywhere in Pakistan falls within the Code's territorial ambit.
els.cite: S.1 PPC — title and extent. Muhammad Aslam v. State PLD 2005 SC 71 confirmed S.1–5 collectively define PPC jurisdiction.

landmark.holding: Muhammad Aslam v. The State PLD 2005 SC 71 — The leading authority on extra-territorial jurisdiction. Facts: A Pakistani citizen committed an offence outside Pakistan and was tried in Pakistan under the PPC. Issue: Whether the PPC applies to offences committed by Pakistani citizens beyond Pakistani territory. Held: The Supreme Court confirmed that under S.4 PPC, the Code has extra-territorial application over Pakistani citizens and persons in the service of Pakistan anywhere in the world. Sections 1–5 collectively define the scope and jurisdiction of the PPC and must be read together as a unified jurisdictional framework.

sq.q: A foreign diplomat commits a theft in Lahore. S.1 says the Code applies throughout Pakistan. Does diplomatic immunity negate PPC jurisdiction entirely?
sq.reveal: Diplomatic immunity operates at the level of enforcement and trial, not substantive criminal law. The PPC technically applies to the act — the theft is an offence under S.378. However Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations 1961 (incorporated in Pakistan law) means the diplomat cannot be prosecuted or arrested. The offence exists in law; enforcement is suspended. S.1 grants jurisdiction; international law restricts its exercise.
sq.cite: S.1 PPC + Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations 1961 — immunity vs jurisdiction

sq.q: Azad Kashmir has its own legal system. Does S.1's phrase 'throughout Pakistan' include Azad Kashmir?
sq.reveal: Azad Kashmir has a special constitutional status. It is not a province of Pakistan and has its own Interim Constitution Act 1974 and its own Azad Jammu & Kashmir Penal Code. S.1's 'throughout Pakistan' applies to the territories constitutionally recognised as Pakistan. This is a live constitutional question with practical implications for cross-border criminal matters.
sq.cite: S.1 PPC — territorial extent. Azad Kashmir constitutional position under Article 257 Constitution 1973

sq.q: The PPC was enacted in 1860 under colonial rule. Is it still valid law in Pakistan post-independence?
sq.reveal: Yes. Under Article 268 of the Constitution of Pakistan 1973, existing laws continue in force until altered, repealed, or amended by a competent authority. The PPC was preserved and adapted by subsequent Pakistani legislation. The Qisas and Diyat Ordinance 1990 was the most significant amendment, replacing colonial punishments with Islamic law provisions for offences against the person.
sq.cite: Article 268 Constitution of Pakistan 1973 — continuity of existing laws

els.name: Offences Within Pakistan
els.sub: S.2 PPC — territorial jurisdiction, every person
els.desc: S.2 establishes the territorial principle: every person guilty of an act contrary to the PPC within Pakistan shall be liable to punishment under this Code and not otherwise. The phrase 'and not otherwise' is critical — it means PPC offences can only be punished under the PPC, not under parallel systems.
els.legal: S.2 applies to every person regardless of nationality or religion — Pakistani citizens, foreign nationals, and stateless persons are all equally liable for offences committed within Pakistan. The phrase 'and not otherwise' prevents parallel prosecution under customary or tribal systems for PPC offences, which has been significant in the context of jirga decisions.
els.cite: S.2 PPC — territorial jurisdiction. Note: Cross-reference CrPC S.177 (place of trial) for procedural aspects of territorial jurisdiction.

sq.q: A jirga (tribal council) orders the killing of a person to settle a family dispute. The killer acts on the jirga's order. Does S.2's 'and not otherwise' prevent the jirga from having authority to punish the killer?
sq.reveal: Yes. S.2's 'and not otherwise' means that where an act constitutes a PPC offence, only the state criminal justice system has authority to try and punish it. A jirga has no legal authority to impose punishment for PPC offences. The Supreme Court has repeatedly held that jirga decisions imposing punishment for offences are illegal. The killer is liable under S.302 PPC; the jirga members may themselves face liability under conspiracy and abetment provisions.
sq.cite: S.2 PPC — 'and not otherwise'. Multiple Supreme Court judgments on illegality of jirga punishment

sq.q: A tourist from the UK commits assault in Karachi and then boards a flight home before arrest. Can Pakistan still prosecute under S.2?
sq.reveal: S.2 establishes jurisdiction based on where the act was committed — the assault in Karachi brings it within S.2 regardless of the accused's nationality. The practical challenge is enforcement: Pakistan can issue an arrest warrant and seek extradition under any applicable extradition treaty with the UK. Jurisdiction exists; enforcement depends on extradition arrangements.
sq.cite: S.2 PPC — applies to every person within Pakistan. Extradition Act 1972 for enforcement

sq.q: S.2 says 'and not otherwise.' A person commits both a PPC offence (robbery) and a violation of the Anti-Terrorism Act in the same act. Which code governs punishment?
sq.reveal: This is the classic special law vs general law problem. The Anti-Terrorism Act 1997 is a special law; the PPC is the general law. The general principle is that a special law prevails over a general law (lex specialis derogat legi generali). However the ATA has its own provision allowing courts to apply PPC offences alongside ATA offences. The 'and not otherwise' in S.2 is qualified by S.5 PPC which preserves special laws.
sq.cite: S.2 and S.5 PPC — interaction with special laws. Anti-Terrorism Act 1997 S.6 and Schedule

els.name: Extra-territorial Offences Triable in Pakistan
els.sub: S.3 PPC — committed abroad, triable here
els.desc: S.3 deals with offences committed outside Pakistan but which are by any Pakistani law triable within Pakistan. Such persons shall be dealt with under the PPC as if the act had been committed within Pakistan. This bridges the territorial and extra-territorial regimes.
els.legal: S.3 operates as a bridge: when another Pakistani law (e.g. the Extradition Act, a bilateral treaty, or special legislation) makes a foreign-committed offence triable in Pakistan, S.3 applies the PPC as the substantive law for punishment. The act is fictional deemed to have occurred in Pakistan. This is the 'protective principle' of jurisdiction — Pakistan protects its interests even for acts abroad.
els.cite: S.3 PPC — quasi-territorial jurisdiction. Cross-reference CrPC S.188 (offences committed outside Pakistan). Extradition Act 1972.

sq.q: A Pakistani national commits fraud in Dubai against Pakistani victims. He returns to Pakistan. Under which section — S.3 or S.4 — does jurisdiction arise?
sq.reveal: Potentially both, but for different reasons. S.4 applies directly because the accused is a Pakistani citizen (active nationality principle). S.3 applies if there is a separate Pakistani law making the Dubai fraud triable here — for example if the Prevention of Electronic Crimes Act covers the fraud. In practice, S.4 is the stronger and more direct basis for jurisdiction. S.3 adds an additional jurisdictional hook when another law creates triability.
sq.cite: S.3 and S.4 PPC — complementary extra-territorial provisions

sq.q: What is the difference between the 'protective principle' in S.3 and the 'nationality principle' in S.4?
sq.reveal: S.3 (protective principle) focuses on triability — Pakistan can try an offence abroad if another Pakistani law creates that triability. It protects Pakistan's legal interests regardless of who committed the act. S.4 (nationality principle/active personality) focuses on who committed the act — Pakistani citizens and state servants are subject to PPC wherever they act. S.3 asks 'is it triable here?' S.4 asks 'is the accused Pakistani?'
sq.cite: S.3 vs S.4 PPC — protective vs nationality principle

sq.q: A person commits cybercrime from a server in Germany targeting Pakistani banks. Can Pakistan assert jurisdiction?
sq.reveal: Yes, potentially under S.3 read with the Prevention of Electronic Crimes Act 2016 (PECA). PECA S.44 gives Pakistani courts jurisdiction over cybercrimes committed outside Pakistan where the effects are felt within Pakistan or where a Pakistani national or entity is affected. S.3 then applies the PPC as the substantive punishment framework. This is exactly the type of cross-reference Mr. Faisal emphasised — the PPC provides the offence and punishment; PECA provides the jurisdictional hook.
sq.cite: S.3 PPC + Prevention of Electronic Crimes Act 2016 S.44 — cyber jurisdiction

els.name: Extra-territorial Jurisdiction
els.sub: S.4 PPC — Pakistani citizens, state servants, vessels
els.desc: S.4 extends the Code to: (1) any citizen of Pakistan or person in the service of Pakistan anywhere in the world; (2) any person on a ship or aircraft registered in Pakistan wherever it may be. This is based on the active nationality principle and flag state jurisdiction.
els.legal: S.4 establishes that being Pakistani — by citizenship or state service — means you carry the PPC with you globally. The flag state jurisdiction for ships and aircraft is based on international law: a Pakistani-registered vessel is Pakistani territory for jurisdictional purposes. The landmark case Muhammad Aslam PLD 2005 SC 71 confirmed this applies to all offences, not just specific ones.
els.cite: S.4 PPC — extra-territorial jurisdiction. Muhammad Aslam v. State PLD 2005 SC 71 — leading authority. Active nationality + flag state principles.

sq.q: A Pakistani diplomat commits murder in London. The UK wants to prosecute. Can Pakistan also assert jurisdiction under S.4? Would this be double jeopardy?
sq.reveal: Pakistan can assert jurisdiction under S.4 — the accused is a Pakistani citizen. Whether double jeopardy applies depends on: (1) whether there is an extradition treaty; (2) the principle of ne bis in idem (not to be tried twice for the same act). Pakistan's Constitution under Article 13 prohibits double punishment for the same offence. If convicted in the UK, a subsequent Pakistani prosecution would face strong double jeopardy arguments. In practice, the states would negotiate which jurisdiction proceeds.
sq.cite: S.4 PPC + Article 13 Constitution 1973 — protection against double punishment

sq.q: A foreign national employed by the Pakistan government as a non-Pakistani employee commits an offence in France. Does S.4's 'person in the service of Pakistan' cover them?
sq.reveal: S.4 covers 'any person in the service of Pakistan' without restricting this to Pakistani nationals. A foreign national employed by the Pakistani state — in an embassy, for instance — would fall within S.4 during the exercise of that service. The jurisdictional rationale is that the Pakistani state is responsible for acts of its servants, regardless of their nationality.
sq.cite: S.4 PPC — 'person in the service of Pakistan' interpreted broadly

sq.q: A crime is committed on a PIA aircraft over international waters. Which country has jurisdiction?
sq.reveal: Pakistan has primary jurisdiction under S.4 — the aircraft is registered in Pakistan and is therefore Pakistani territory for jurisdictional purposes under the flag state principle and the Tokyo Convention on Offences Committed on Board Aircraft 1963. The country of departure and destination may also claim jurisdiction. Pakistan's claim under S.4 is however the strongest and most direct.
sq.cite: S.4 PPC — flag state jurisdiction. Tokyo Convention 1963 on Offences on Board Aircraft

els.name: Special Laws Not Displaced
els.sub: S.5 PPC — military and special laws preserved
els.desc: S.5 is the negation provision — the PPC's own savings clause. Nothing in the PPC is intended to repeal, vary, suspend or affect: (1) Military laws: Army Act, Navy Ordinance, Air Force Act; (2) Special laws: Anti-Terrorism Act 1997, Control of Narcotic Substances Act 1997, Prevention of Electronic Crimes Act 2016. Where a special law applies, the PPC does not displace it. However the PPC and a special law may operate simultaneously on the same act.
els.legal: S.5 operates as the lex specialis carve-out. The major statutes it explicitly saves fall into two categories. Military laws: Army Act 1952, Pakistan Navy Ordinance 1961, Pakistan Air Force Act 1953 — these govern mutiny, desertion, and military discipline exclusively. Special and local laws: Anti-Terrorism Act 1997 (ATA), Control of Narcotic Substances Act 1997 (CNSA), Prevention of Electronic Crimes Act 2016 (PECA), Anti-Money Laundering Act 2010. Each of these has its own offence definitions, evidentiary standards, and punishment regimes. S.5 ensures the PPC does not repeal, vary, or interfere with any of them. Critically however, S.5 does not create exclusivity — a single act may simultaneously engage both the PPC and a special law, and courts regularly apply PPC offences alongside ATA charges in terrorism prosecutions.
els.cite: S.5 PPC — savings clause. Military laws: Army Act 1952, Navy Ordinance 1961, Air Force Act 1953. Special laws: Anti-Terrorism Act 1997, CNSA 1997, PECA 2016, Anti-Money Laundering Act 2010. Mr. Faisal Awais explicitly named ATA and CNSA as primary examples in class.

sq.q: A soldier commits murder while on active duty. The Army Act covers his conduct. Does the PPC apply at all?
sq.reveal: S.5 saves the Army Act, so military disciplinary proceedings under the Army Act are not affected by the PPC. However murder is also a PPC offence, and the Supreme Court has held that military courts under the Army Act have jurisdiction over PPC offences committed by service personnel. The interaction is complex: the Army Act tribunal can try the murder, applying PPC punishment provisions. S.5 saves the Army Act's jurisdiction; it does not exclude PPC substantive law entirely.
sq.cite: S.5 PPC + Army Act 1952. Multiple Supreme Court judgments on military court jurisdiction over PPC offences

sq.q: A person is charged under both S.302 PPC (murder) and the Anti-Terrorism Act. S.5 saves special laws. Does this mean S.302 PPC is displaced by the ATA?
sq.reveal: Not necessarily displaced — concurrent application is possible. S.5 saves the ATA from being affected by the PPC, but this does not mean the PPC is excluded. Courts have held that where an act constitutes both a PPC offence and an ATA offence, the ATA court (Anti-Terrorism Court) has jurisdiction but may apply PPC offences as scheduled offences under the ATA. S.5 prevents conflict; it does not create exclusive jurisdiction in the special law.
sq.cite: S.5 PPC + Anti-Terrorism Act 1997 S.6. ATC jurisdiction over scheduled PPC offences

sq.q: A person commits cybercrime. PECA 2016 covers this specifically. Is the PPC entirely irrelevant?
sq.reveal: No. PECA 2016 creates new offences and punishments for cyber-specific acts. However where a cybercrime also constitutes a PPC offence — for example cybercrime used to commit fraud (S.420 PPC) or threaten (S.506 PPC) — the PPC provisions apply concurrently or are incorporated by PECA. S.5 prevents the PPC from interfering with PECA's specific provisions; it does not exclude PPC offences that overlap with cyber conduct.
sq.cite: S.5 PPC + PECA 2016. Concurrent application of PPC and special laws
